Farn Polrrrcnl PRACTTcEs Courr,rrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 29,2007
Amy Northam
Legal Analyst, Legal Services Dept.
San Juan Unified School District
P.O.Box477
Carmichael, California 9 5 608
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-07-042
Dear Ms. Northam:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of San Juan unified
!"1'9:l District Superintendent Steven W. inoch, regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the ..Act,). I
QUESTION
May San Juan Unified School District Superintendent Enoch accept a payment for
travel from a software vendor in order to discussihe vendor's contract with the school
district?
CONCLUSION
No, unless the payment for travel does not exceed the gift limits of the Act.
FACTS
The school diskict has an existing contract with a software vendor who is
headquartered in Virginia. This software controls the district,s student information, a
vital and essential component of district operations. Significant "o*pti"atiorr. t urr.
arisen with this vendor and their software. The superinlendent and the vendor wish to
meet face-to-face to discuss the issues. It is more iost effective for the vendor to arrange
Tdp?v for the superintendent (ust one person) to travel to the vendor,s heaoquarters
than for the vendor to travel to the school district offices. As such, the vendor has
requested to pay for travel expenses for Superintendent Enoch. The school district,s
I Government code sections 81000-91014. commissionregulations appear attitle2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-07-042
Page No. 2
confract with the vendor does not address travel expenses. you wish to know if the
vendor may pay for the superintendent's fravel to virginia.
In addition, durilg our telephone conversation of March 8, z}Oz,you stated that
the superintendent is a designated employee of the school district, and the vendor is a
private, for-profit entity, and it is not an educational instifution. you also stated that your
district's conflict of interest code would require the superintendent to report gt, Aom
this vendor.
ANALYSIS
Gifts, Generally
The Act imposes different obligations on public officials regarding the receipt of
gfts.
1' Gift Reportins. 
- When an agency's conflict of interest code requires a
designated employee to report gifts from specified persons o" it " official,s
statement of economic interests, gtrfts from each person must be reported when
they amount to $50 or more during the applicabli reporting period. (Sections
87 3 02(b) and 87 207(aX I ).)
2' Gift Limit. Section 89503 imposes a gift limit on designated employees of
state and local government agencies. (Section 39503(;).) SpecificailS
section 89503(c) provides that no designated employee-of u'rtut" or Iocal
govemment agency may accept gifts from any single source in any calendar
year in excess of the gift limit if the official is required to report the receipt of
gifts from that source on his or her statement of etonomic lnterests. fne
current gift limit is $390. (Regutation tgg4}.z)
3' Conflicts qf Interest. When an official receives gifts aggregating $390 more
from the same source within the 12 months prior to when tt " otf,"iut
participates in a governmental decision that has a financial impact on that
sollrce, the official may have a conflict of interest under the ect. (Sections
87100 and 87103(e).) 
\
Because Superintendent Enoch is a designated employee of the San Juan Unified
school District, a local government agency,*a giru mm mL software vendor are
reportable on Superintendent Enoch's statement of economic interests, tt " ubo*
provisions apply to him.


File No. A-07-042
Page No. 3
Gifts of Travel
In general, a public official's havel paid for by a source whose gifts are reportable
on the ofEcial's statement of economic interests is subject to gift limits and reportable on
the statement of economic interests.
Section 82028(a) defines a..gift,, as:
"[A]nypaymerrt that confers a personal benefit on the recipient,to
the extent that consideration of equal or greaier value is noi
received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything
of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular-courie
of business to members of the public without regard to official
status . . .." (Emphasis added.)z
You stated in your letter and during our telephone conversation of March g,2007,
that the software vendor, a private, for-profit entity, has offered to pay fo, th;
superintendent's flight to Virgini4 where the vendor is located. the purpose of the trip is
for the superintendent and the vendor to "meet face-to-face." This tavel'is a personal
benefit !o the superintendent and his services in the meeting with the vendor are rendered
on behalf of the dishict and not as legal consideration to the vendor. Therefore, unless an
exception applies, the value of the travel is_a gift to the superintendent and subject to the
reporting requirements, glft limits and conflict of interestirovisions r* forduUorr".
Section 89506 provides that payments, advances, or reimbursements for travel,
(including actual transportation and related lodging and subsistence) that is reasonably
related to a legislative or govemmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
international policy, are not prohibited or limited if erther of the foffowint appiy,
"(1) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected
state ofEcer, local elected officeholder, candidite fo, elected state
office or local elected officg an individual specified in section
87200, member of a state board or commissi,or, o, designated
ernployee of a state or local government agerrcy,the loJging and
subsistence expenses are limited to the day immeaiatel/prieding
the day of, and the day immediatery following the speech, and thJ-
travel is within the United States.
2 T\e emphasized language, "that confers a personal benefit on the recipien!,, was added to
section82028(a)bvtheLegisl{urein1997. (stats. l9vl,cb.450(s.B. 124),z,eff.sept. 24,1997.) tnthe
Yee Adice Letter, No' 4-98-19? (copy enclosed), we advised that a palment may confer a personal benefit
to an official even where it facilitates the conduct of govemmental business.


File No. A-07-042
Page No. 4
*Q)The travel is provided by a government, a govemmental
agency, a foreign government, a govemmental authority, a bona
fide public or private educational institution. ..u rrorprofit
organizatron that is exempt from taxation under Seciion 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code. . ..,,
Neither the stated pu{pose of the trip, nor the donor offering to pay for the trip
meets the above factors in Section 89506. Thereforg under the faJts yo.rirr" provided,
the vendor's payment for airline transportation and any other havel c6sts, inclu-cling '
meals and accommodations, would be deemed a gift to the superintendent, zubjectio the
reporting and gift limits of the Act.
Gifts to an Agency
In some instances, such a payment may be considered a gift to an agency. Where
it is clear that the payment would othenrise be a gift to one o, mbre public offi"iulr, th"
agency involved may resort to Regulation 18944.2. Compliance witir that regulation will
result in characterizationof the grft as one to the agency.
Regulation 18944.2, in pertinent part, states a gift may be deerned a gift to a
public agency, and not a gft to apublic official, if attitthe Allo*irg requiiements are
met:
"(1) The agency receives and controls thepayment.
"(2)Thepayment is used for official agencybusiness.
"(3) The age*cy, in its sole discretion, determines the specific
official or officials who shalr use the payment. However, the donor
may identifu a specific pulpose for the agency's use of the
payment, so long as the donor does not designate the specific
official or officials who may use the payment.
"(4) The agency memorializes the payment in a written public
record which embodies the requirements of subdivisions (a)(l) to
(aX3) ofthis regulation . . ..,,
You stated that the software vendor has offered to pay for the superintendent,s
flight to Virginia. If the donor is limiting the use of the payment to a single specified
employeg all the requirements ofRegulation 18944.2 arenot satisfied. it6, tt "
payment for the superintendent's flight cannot be deemed a grft to the school district
unless the payment is not limited to his use. Otherwise, it is a git to the superintendent,
subject to gift limits and the reporting and conflict of interest provisions of theActj --'
3 The current gift limit is $390 from a single source in a single calendar year. (Regulation
18940.2, enclosed.) To the extent an official receives a gift(s) that ex-ceeds the gift ti-it, *"tfr" official


File No. A-07-042
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn
Counsel, Legal Division
ERjsl
I:\Adviceltrs\07-042
Enclosures
prefers not to disclose, regulation 18943 provides that an official mayreimburse the donor forthe value of
a gift. If the reimbursement is made within 30 days ofreceipt, the official has not received a gift.
Alternatively
an official may, within 30 days, reimburse the donor the amount that exceeds the gift limit or
that brings the value of the gift below the $50 reporting threshold. Gifts of $50 ormore must be reported
on an official's statement of economic interests.
- 
In addition, please be aware that Section 87100 et seq. of the Act requires that public officials who
have received gifts of $390 o1_mgre from a single source must disqualify themselve. to-* -y
governmental decision that will foreseeably and materially affect tie donor.

QUESTION: May San Juan Unified School District Superintendent Enoch accept a payment for
travel from a software vendor in order to discussihe vendor's contract with the school
district?

CONCLUSION: No, unless the payment for travel does not exceed the gift limits of the Act.
